summary: 2b continues the Gemara's analysis of the gezerah shavah that sources the seven-day separation rule. Before accepting that 'tzivah' in the milu'im verse links to the heifer's 'tzivah,' the Gemara systematically disqualifies two other 'tzivah' candidates: the one in the Yom Kippur passage (it appears after the service, not before) and the 'tzavoto' form in the offerings verse (different conjugation). A challenge from Rabbi Yishmael's school - that different verb forms can form a gezerah shavah - is answered by the rule that identical forms take priority when available. The Gemara then narrows 'le-chaper' (to atone) to exclude general offerings (daily, no fixed time), Festivals (three times a year), any single Festival (a seven-day service, not a one-day service), and Shemini Atzeret (preceded by sanctified days). A kal va-chomer is proposed and rejected by both Rav Mesharshiyya and Rav Ashi.

display.title: Why not use Yom Kippur's 'tzivah'? Before vs. after performance
display.oneLine: The Gemara proposes using Yom Kippur's 'tzivah' for the gezerah shavah, then rejects it - that 'tzivah' appears after the service, not before; similarly 'tzavoto' is a different conjugation.
display.shortSummary: Two alternative 'tzivah' candidates are disqualified: Yom Kippur's 'tzivah' follows the service instead of preceding it (post-facto cannot link to pre-facto), and the offerings passage uses 'tzavoto' which is a different verb form than 'tzivah.'
display.whats: The Gemara has established that 'tzivah' in the milu'im verse links to the parah adumah. But why not link it instead to 'tzivah' in the Yom Kippur passage (Leviticus 16:34), and derive only the Yom Kippur seven-day rule? Answer: that 'tzivah' appears after the Yom Kippur service was performed, while the milu'im 'tzivah' appears before. A gezerah shavah must connect functionally parallel contexts. Similarly, the offerings passage uses 'tzavoto' (a different conjugation), which cannot pair with 'tzivah.'
display.hint: The before/after distinction seems subtle. Why does the position of 'tzivah' relative to the service matter for a gezerah shavah?

learning.learnerQuestion: The Torah mentions 'tzivah' near both Yom Kippur and offerings. Why can't we derive the separation rule from those contexts instead?
learning.coreTension: When multiple instances of a word are available for a gezerah shavah, are they all equally valid, or must the analogy connect structurally parallel situations?
learning.coreMove: A gezerah shavah requires not only the same word but also the same functional position - here, a command that precedes performance.
learning.resolution: Yom Kippur's 'tzivah' is post-facto and cannot link to the pre-performance 'tzivah' of the inauguration. The 'tzavoto' form is a different verb form entirely.

takeaway.text: A gezerah shavah requires not just the same word but also equivalent functional context. A command placed after an act cannot link to a command placed before an act.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Gemara applies progressive filtration to candidate pairs for gezerot shavot.

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara isn't just collecting identical words - it's requiring that the analogy be structurally coherent. 'Tzivah' after the fact and 'tzivah' before the fact aren't the same legal speech-act.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think any shared word triggers a gezerah shavah. The Gemara here shows that position and context are also criteria.
learning.memoryAnchor: Yom Kippur's 'tzivah' appears in 'and he did as the Lord commanded' - the command is recalled after the fact. You cannot derive a before-the-service rule from a command recorded after the service.

argumentFlow.label: Propose Yom Kippur's tzivah
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Why not link 'tzivah' from the inauguration to 'tzivah' from the Yom Kippur passage and derive only the Yom Kippur seven-day separation?

argumentFlow.label: Before vs. after
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We derive 'tzivah' that precedes performance from 'tzivah' that precedes performance; we do not derive 'tzivah' that follows performance from 'tzivah' that precedes it.

argumentFlow.label: Propose offerings' tzavoto
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Why not link 'tzivah' to 'tzavoto' in the offerings passage, deriving that any priest sacrificing a communal offering must separate?

argumentFlow.label: Different verb forms
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We derive tzivah from tzivah - identical form - and not tzavoto from tzivah.

misconceptions.misconception: Any occurrence of the same word in the Torah can create a valid gezerah shavah.
misconceptions.correction: The word must appear in a functionally equivalent position - before performance, not after. Position and context are criteria alongside word identity.

visualizableElements.item: Timeline showing 'tzivah' in YK passage (after service) vs. 'tzivah' in inauguration (before service) - same word, opposite positions
Table comparing two 'tzivah' candidates: YK (post-facto) and 'tzavoto' (different form) - both disqualified for different reasons

quizSeeds.question: Why does the Gemara reject using Yom Kippur's 'tzivah' for the gezerah shavah?
quizSeeds.answer: That 'tzivah' appears after the Yom Kippur service was performed ('and he did as the Lord commanded'), while the milu'im 'tzivah' precedes performance. A gezerah shavah must connect functionally parallel contexts.

quizSeeds.question: Why can't 'tzavoto' in the offerings passage link to 'tzivah' in the inauguration?
quizSeeds.answer: We derive 'tzivah' from 'tzivah' - identical verbal form only. 'Tzavoto' is a different conjugation and cannot pair with 'tzivah' when the identical form is available.

display.title: Challenge from Rabbi Yishmael's school: can different verb forms link?
display.oneLine: The school of Rabbi Yishmael derived a gezerah shavah from 'veshav' and 'uva' - two different verbs. Doesn't that mean 'tzavoto' can link to 'tzivah'? Answer: only when no identical form is available.
display.shortSummary: Rabbi Yishmael's school linked 've-shav' (returning) to 'u-va' (coming) in leprous-house laws - two different verb roots. If they can form a gezerah shavah, why can't 'tzavoto' link to 'tzivah'? The Gemara answers: different forms are only used when no identical form exists. Since 'tzivah' is available in the heifer passage, that identical form takes precedence.
display.whats: The school of Rabbi Yishmael derived a halakha from 've-shav' (and he returned) linked to 'u-va' (and he came) - two completely different verbs. If that works, why can't 'tzivah' link to 'tzavoto'? The answer introduces a hierarchy rule: you must use the closest available match. When 'tzivah' is available in the heifer passage, you have to use that exact form. Rabbi Yishmael's school used different verbs only because no identical form was available in the leprous-house context.
display.hint: What does this rule reveal about how the Sages thought a gezerah shavah derives its authority?

learning.learnerQuestion: If Rabbi Yishmael's school used two completely different verbs in a gezerah shavah, why is 'tzavoto' disqualified for being slightly different from 'tzivah'?
learning.coreTension: The school of Rabbi Yishmael's precedent seems to show that different forms can link; but that precedent also contains its own limiting principle.
learning.coreMove: The hierarchy rule: identical forms take priority over similar forms. The Yishmael precedent applies only in the absence of an identical match.
learning.resolution: Different forms are acceptable only where identical forms do not exist. The availability of 'tzivah' in the heifer passage mandates using it, not the near-match 'tzavoto.'

takeaway.text: Among available verbal analogy candidates, identical forms always take priority over similar forms. A near-match is only used when no exact match exists.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Gemara establishes a preference ordering for gezerah shavah candidates.

learning.ahaMoment: Rabbi Yishmael's school's own precedent is what generates the limiting rule. The challenge defeats itself: their methodology already contains a priority principle that explains why 'tzavoto' cannot work here.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may take the Yishmael precedent as a blanket permission for near-matches. The Gemara shows it is the exception, not the rule.
learning.memoryAnchor: Different verb roots can pair in a gezerah shavah - but only as a last resort. 'Tzavoto' fails here not because it is too different, but because 'tzivah' is available. The fallback rule only activates when the exact match is absent.

argumentFlow.label: Yishmael school's precedent
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: What practical difference is there? The school of Rabbi Yishmael derived a gezerah shavah from 've-shav' (Lev. 14:39) and 'u-va' (Lev. 14:44) - two completely different roots. Clearly different forms can link.

argumentFlow.label: Priority of identical forms
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: That rule (using different forms) applies only where no identical form is available. Where an identical form is available, we derive from the identical form.

misconceptions.misconception: R. Yishmael's school's precedent shows that different verb forms can always be linked.
misconceptions.correction: Their precedent applies only when no identical form is available - it is the exception, not the rule. The availability of 'tzivah' mandates using it over any near-match.

visualizableElements.item: Hierarchy diagram: identical verbal form (priority) -> similar form (fallback) -> different roots (last resort only)
R. Yishmael's leprous-house precedent: 've-shav' and 'u-va' - different roots that successfully link, showing the fallback rule in action

quizSeeds.question: What precedent does R. Yishmael's school offer, and how does it challenge the rejection of 'tzavoto'?
quizSeeds.answer: R. Yishmael's school linked 've-shav' to 'u-va' in leprous-house laws - completely different verb roots. If different roots can form a gezerah shavah, why can't 'tzavoto' link with 'tzivah'?

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara answer R. Yishmael's school's challenge?
quizSeeds.answer: The R. Yishmael precedent applies only when no identical form is available. Since 'tzivah' is available in the heifer passage, that identical form must be used. Different forms are a fallback, not a first choice.

display.title: Does 'to atone' include general offerings or Festivals?
display.oneLine: The phrase 'to atone' in the inauguration passage could mean atonement offerings generally or Festivals - both are excluded: daily offerings lack a fixed annual time, and Festivals occur three times a year, not once.
display.shortSummary: The Gemara analyzes the 'le-chaper' (to atone) link in the inauguration verse. Could it include all atonement offerings? Practically one could require the whole beit av to separate. But a principle limits the derivation to time-fixed, once-a-year events. Could it include Festival service? Festivals happen three times a year, not once - so they too are excluded.
display.whats: The Gemara now works through 'le-chaper' (to atone) and asks what it includes. Could it mean any atonement offering? The Gemara first tries a practical objection - we can't know in advance which kohen will serve - but that gets shot down: just require the whole beit av to separate. So a principle is invoked instead: we derive only what has a fixed annual time, which excludes daily offerings. Could it mean Festival offerings? No - the milu'im was a one-time event, so we derive only what recurs exactly once a year. Festivals happen three times a year.
display.hint: The Gemara uses two different exclusion principles for offerings vs. Festivals. Why doesn't a single principle cover both?

learning.learnerQuestion: Why can't the separation rule apply to all atonement offerings, or at least to Festival service?
learning.coreTension: The 'to atone' phrase in the inauguration is broad - how to limit it without an explicit verse? Two different principles are needed for two different candidate categories.
learning.coreMove: Daily offerings are excluded by 'fixed time'; Festivals are excluded by 'once a year.' The criteria are distinct because the failure modes differ.
learning.resolution: The inauguration was a unique, once-a-year event with a fixed date. Only Yom Kippur shares both features simultaneously.

takeaway.text: When deriving rules by analogy, every feature of the source case must be matched. The inauguration was both time-fixed and once-a-year; daily offerings fail on the first, Festivals on the second.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Gemara excludes candidates one at a time by showing which feature of the inauguration each candidate lacks.

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara could have used 'once a year' to exclude daily offerings too, but it uses 'fixed time' instead - a broader and more immediate criterion. This suggests the Gemara is building the argument in the most efficient order.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may wonder why the practical objection fails. The Gemara takes it seriously enough to rebut it before invoking the principle.
learning.memoryAnchor: Daily offerings: no annual date, excluded by 'fixed time.' Festivals: three times a year, excluded by 'once a year.' Two deficiencies, two different criteria. The Gemara uses the most precise tool available for each case.

argumentFlow.label: le-chaper = all atonement offerings
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'To atone' could refer to any atonement offering - requiring separation before all such service.

argumentFlow.label: Practical objection
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Do we know in advance which priest will serve? We'd need to know that to impose separation.

argumentFlow.label: Require the whole beit av
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We could require the entire beit av to separate - they rotate by family and we know the schedule.

argumentFlow.label: Fixed time principle
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We derive a time-fixed matter (the inauguration) from another time-fixed matter (Yom Kippur). Daily offerings occur every day - they have no fixed annual time.

argumentFlow.label: le-chaper = Festival service
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: And say 'to atone' refers to Festival service, which does have a fixed time.

argumentFlow.label: Once-a-year principle
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We derive what occurs once a year (the inauguration) from what occurs once a year (Yom Kippur). Festivals occur three times a year.

misconceptions.misconception: The practical objection (which kohen will serve?) defeats the proposal to include all atonement offerings.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara explicitly refutes this objection: the mishmar and beit av schedule is known in advance. The exclusion is on principle (no fixed annual date), not practicality.

visualizableElements.item: Elimination flowchart: atonement offerings (no fixed annual date, excluded) -> Festivals (three times a year, excluded) -> only Yom Kippur remains
Beit av rotation schedule: each priestly family knows its service week in advance, refuting the practical objection

quizSeeds.question: Why can't the seven-day separation rule extend to priests who sacrifice atonement offerings generally?
quizSeeds.answer: Daily atonement offerings have no fixed annual time, while the inauguration was a time-fixed event. The Gemara derives a time-fixed matter (inauguration) from another time-fixed matter (Yom Kippur) only.

quizSeeds.question: Why are Festivals excluded from the gezerah shavah scope via 'le-chaper' (to atone)?
quizSeeds.answer: The inauguration was a once-a-year event. Festivals occur three times a year and therefore cannot be derived from a once-a-year model.

display.title: Excluding one Festival and Shemini Atzeret: the decisive principle
display.oneLine: Even a single Festival is excluded - Festivals span seven days while the inauguration was preparation for a single culminating day. Seven-for-one cannot derive seven-for-seven.
display.shortSummary: What if one Festival qualifies - perhaps Passover (listed first in the Torah) or Sukkot (most offerings)? The Gemara cuts this off with a structural principle: the inauguration involved seven days of preparation for one culminating day. But Festival service itself spans seven days. You cannot derive a 'seven days for one day' rule and apply it to a 'seven days for seven days' situation.
display.whats: What if one Festival qualifies - perhaps Passover (listed first in the Torah) or Sukkot (most offerings)? The Gemara cuts this off with a structural principle: the milu'im involved seven days of preparation for a single culminating day of service. Yom Kippur matches that exactly. But the Festivals themselves span seven days - you cannot derive a 'seven for one' rule and apply it to a 'seven for seven' situation.
display.hint: What does the Gemara mean by 'seven for one'? And why does the duration of the service being derived actually matter?

learning.learnerQuestion: What's wrong with requiring separation before just one Festival, say Passover?
learning.coreTension: A Festival occurs only once a year, matching that criterion - but the structural shape of Festival service is fundamentally different from Yom Kippur.
learning.coreMove: The analogy requires not just temporal frequency but structural matching: seven days of preparation for a single-day service.
learning.resolution: Festival service spans seven days; the inauguration's eight-day sequence culminated in one day of service. Only Yom Kippur, a single day of service, matches the structural model.

takeaway.text: A gezerah shavah or hekkesh must match not only the temporal frequency of the source case but its structural shape. Preparation for a seven-day service is categorically different from preparation for a one-day service.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Gemara adds a structural dimension to the analogy criteria.

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara is precise: it is not 'seven days for Passover' that's wrong - it is 'seven days of preparation for seven days of service.' The inauguration model is specifically one of intensive short preparation for a single peak moment.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may not see why the duration of the Festival matters if the separation only happens before the first day.
learning.memoryAnchor: 'Seven days of preparation for a one-day service' is the structural model. A seven-day Festival cannot match it, because then it would be seven-for-seven, not seven-for-one. The shape of the preparation must match the shape of what it prepares for.

argumentFlow.label: One Festival as the source
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: And say that the separation is required before one specific Festival - either Passover (listed first) or Sukkot (most commandments).

argumentFlow.label: Seven-for-one principle
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We derive sequestering for seven days prior to a one-day service from sequestering for seven days prior to a one-day service. We do not derive sequestering before a seven-day service from sequestering before a one-day service.

misconceptions.misconception: Passover is excluded only because Festivals happen three times a year.
misconceptions.correction: That criterion applies to Festivals collectively (sugya 3). A single Festival is excluded by a deeper structural mismatch: Festival service itself spans seven days, not one. The preparation shape must match the service shape.

visualizableElements.item: Structural comparison: inauguration (7 days ordinary prep + 1 culminating service day) vs. Festival (7 days of continuous service) - the shapes do not match

quizSeeds.question: Even though Passover occurs only once a year, why can't the seven-day separation apply before it?
quizSeeds.answer: The inauguration was seven days of preparation for a single culminating day of service. Festivals span seven days of service themselves. You cannot derive a 'seven-for-one' rule and apply it to a 'seven-for-seven' situation.

quizSeeds.question: What does 'prisha shivah le-yom echad' mean in this context?
quizSeeds.answer: Separating for seven days as preparation for a single-day culminating service. The inauguration and Yom Kippur both match this model; Festivals do not.

display.title: Shemini Atzeret excluded; kal va-chomer rejected by Rav Mesharshiyya and Rav Ashi
display.oneLine: Shemini Atzeret is excluded because it is preceded by seven holy days, while the inauguration was preceded by ordinary days. The resulting kal va-chomer is rejected by Rav Mesharshiyya ('like this day') and Rav Ashi (the primary Festival itself needs no separation).
display.shortSummary: Shemini Atzeret looks like the ideal candidate: single day, preceded by seven days. But those preceding days are the holy days of Sukkot - not ordinary weekdays like the inauguration's run-up. A kal va-chomer (surely what's more holy needs more preparation) is proposed and doubly rejected: Rav Mesharshiyya invokes 'like this day' (precisely like the inauguration); Rav Ashi adds that since the primary Festival (Sukkot) needs no separation, its appendage certainly cannot be stricter.
display.whats: Shemini Atzeret looks like the perfect candidate: seven days of preparation (Sukkot) for one culminating day of service. The Gemara excludes it on the grounds that Yom Kippur and the milu'im were both preceded by ordinary, unconsecrated days. Shemini Atzeret is preceded by seven holy days of Sukkot - a fundamentally different situation. A kal va-chomer is then proposed: if a less-sanctified lead-up still requires separation, surely a more-sanctified one does too. Rav Mesharshiyya shuts this down: the verse says 'like this day' - exactly as the milu'im, no more. Rav Ashi adds a practical clincher: Sukkot itself (the primary Festival) requires no separation, so its appendage certainly cannot be stricter.
display.hint: Rav Ashi's argument works even for those who hold Shemini Atzeret is fully independent. What does that tell us about how he is reasoning?

learning.learnerQuestion: Shemini Atzeret is preceded by seven days just like the inauguration was. Why isn't it the perfect case to derive from?
learning.coreTension: The seven days before Shemini Atzeret are the holy days of Sukkot - the opposite of the unconsecrated days preceding the inauguration. More sanctity in the lead-up paradoxically disqualifies the case rather than strengthening it.
learning.coreMove: The verse 'like this day' limits the derivation to cases structurally identical to the inauguration. Rav Ashi adds a secondary argument from the internal logic of the Festival system.
learning.resolution: The inauguration was preceded by ordinary days; any case preceded by holy days is excluded. The kal va-chomer cannot override an explicit textual limitation. Sukkot (seven days) needing no separation further proves that what follows it cannot be stricter.

takeaway.text: A textual limitation ('like this day') can block a formally valid kal va-chomer. Additionally, a corollary cannot be stricter than the primary case it depends on.

reasoningPattern.notes: Classic case where a textual constraint takes precedence over an a-fortiori argument.

learning.ahaMoment: Rav Ashi's argument is elegant: he sidesteps the dispute about whether Shemini Atzeret is independent or an extension. Either way, Sukkot needs no separation - and that makes Shemini Atzeret's claim to require separation untenable on any reading.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may find the kal va-chomer compelling and wonder why 'like this day' overrides it. The Gemara is applying a rule that explicit verse-based limitations trump inference.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rav Ashi's rule: if the main Festival needs no preparation, the day appended to it cannot require preparation. The less cannot be stricter than the more. The verse 'as on this day' then closes the door on the kal va-chomer entirely.

argumentFlow.label: Shemini Atzeret as candidate
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: And say the separation is required before Shemini Atzeret - it is also sequestering for seven days before a one-day service.

argumentFlow.label: Sanctity of preceding days
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We derive a case not preceded by sanctity (inauguration) from a case not preceded by sanctity (Yom Kippur). Shemini Atzeret is preceded by seven holy days of Sukkot.

argumentFlow.label: Kal va-chomer proposed
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If a case without preceding sanctity requires separation, surely a case with preceding sanctity requires it all the more!

argumentFlow.label: Rav Mesharshiyya: 'like this day'
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Mesharshiyya
argumentFlow.text: The verse says 'as the Lord commanded to do on this day' - separation is required only for a case like the inauguration exactly, not for any case with more sanctity.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Ashi: the primary Festival needs no separation
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Ashi
argumentFlow.text: Is there any matter where the primary Festival requires no separation but what is secondary to it does? Sukkot (seven days) needs no separation; Shemini Atzeret (its adjunct) cannot be stricter than the primary.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Ashi: primary Festival needs no separation

misconceptions.misconception: Rav Ashi's argument assumes Shemini Atzeret is not an independent holiday.
misconceptions.correction: Rav Ashi explicitly works even for those who hold Shemini Atzeret is fully independent. Either way, Sukkot (the primary Festival) needs no separation, so its appendage cannot be stricter.

visualizableElements.item: Three-case comparison: inauguration (ordinary days -> 1 service day), Yom Kippur (ordinary days -> 1 service day), Shemini Atzeret (7 holy days of Sukkot -> 1 service day) - the third does not match
Rav Ashi's argument: Sukkot (7 days, no separation required) -> Shemini Atzeret cannot be stricter than its primary

quizSeeds.question: Why is Shemini Atzeret excluded from the gezerah shavah, despite being a single-day service preceded by seven days?
quizSeeds.answer: Its preceding seven days are the holy days of Sukkot, not ordinary unconsecrated weekdays like those preceding the inauguration. The model requires ordinary days preceding the service.

quizSeeds.question: What is Rav Ashi's argument against requiring separation before Shemini Atzeret?
quizSeeds.answer: Sukkot itself (seven days, the primary Festival) requires no seven-day separation. A secondary appended day cannot be stricter than the primary Festival it follows.

glossary.en: Verbal analogy: a Talmudic derivation that transfers rules between two passages sharing an identical or near-identical word or phrase. Must be received by tradition, and identical forms take priority over similar ones when both are available.

A fortiori argument: if a less stringent case requires X, then a more stringent case certainly does. One of the Thirteen Principles of R. Yishmael, but can be overridden by an explicit textual limitation.

The one-day holiday immediately after Sukkot. Debate exists whether it is an independent holiday or the closing day of Sukkot - but either way, being preceded by the seven holy days of Sukkot disqualifies it from the gezerah shavah comparison.

The Temple priestly rotation: 24 teams (mishmarot) each serving one week at a time, subdivided by family units (batei av) serving specific days. The Gemara notes this schedule could theoretically allow advance identification of which kohen needs to separate.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi addresses the Gemara's question: why not use the 'tzivah of offerings' to derive separation, requiring every kohen who serves in the Temple to separate seven days beforehand?

Rashi introduces the school of R. Yishmael's verbal analogy from the leprous-house laws, where 've-shav' (the priest shall return) and 'u-va' (the priest shall come) are linked: 'this is returning, and this is coming.'

Rashi spells out the substance of that gezerah shavah: just as 'returning' means the priest removes, scrapes, and plasters, so too 'coming' requires the same procedure - proving different verb roots can form a gezerah shavah.

Rashi explains the Gemara's proposal: 'atonement of offerings' means atonement offerings for individuals, since phrases like 'and the priest shall atone for him' appear many times in the Torah in that context.
Rashi quotes the Gemara's practical objection: 'and the priest shall atone for him' - but do we know in advance which kohen will end up performing that service?
Rashi explains the force of that question: priests serve by lottery, so we don't know in advance who will sacrifice a given offering.
Rashi cross-references the lottery discussion that appears later in the tractate (22a), where the Temple lottery system is described in detail.
Rashi notes a textual variant: the Gemara reads 'they say: Why not?' as the response to the lottery objection, rejecting it.
Rashi explains the proposed solution: require the entire patrilineal family (beit av) of the priestly watch to separate seven days before their assigned service week.
Rashi glosses 'almah lo' as a rhetorical question expressing astonishment: you cannot say there is no way to know - the schedule is known in advance.
Rashi continues: you could indeed require separation, because for each beit av of the priestly watch, the days of service are divided and assigned.
Rashi elaborates on the structure: each beit av is assigned specific days within the week of their mishmar's service.
Rashi continues: since each beit av knows its day, they could all separate seven days beforehand - so the practical objection fails.
Rashi concludes the solution: each beit av would separate the seven days leading up to their designated service day.
Rashi explains the principle that excludes daily offerings: 'a matter that has a fixed time for it' - Yom Kippur has a fixed date, and we derive from the inauguration, which also had a fixed date.
Rashi cites the verse proving the inauguration was time-fixed: Exodus 40 - 'On the first day of the first month' - establishing a specific date for the Tabernacle's consecration.
Rashi continues quoting: 'you shall erect [the Tabernacle]' - referring to the specific calendar date in Exodus 40.
Rashi notes: that erection was the eighth day of the inauguration ceremonies, proving the inauguration concluded on a fixed calendar date.
Rashi explains why the inauguration counts as 'once a year': it occurred on a specific day in a specific year, establishing it as an annual-cycle, time-anchored event.
Rashi pivots to the next proposal: 'If [we say it applies to] Passover' - the Gemara raises the possibility that one Festival might qualify.
Rashi explains the case for Passover: one could argue that the verse is speaking about Passover specifically, since Torah lists it first among the festivals.
Rashi continues: the Torah consistently places Passover first when listing the festivals, suggesting a kind of primacy.
Rashi adds the alternative argument for Sukkot: one could prefer Sukkot instead, since it has more associated commandments than the other festivals.
Rashi lists Sukkot's numerous commandments: the sukkah itself, lulav, willow branch, water libation - making Sukkot the festival with the most mitzvot.
Rashi glosses the key phrase: 'for one day' means the eight days of Sukkot culminate in Shemini Atzeret as a single-day climax, just as the inauguration culminated in one day.
Rashi clarifies: 'And say Shemini' in the Gemara refers to Shemini Atzeret, the Eighth Day of Assembly immediately following Sukkot.
Rashi explains Rav Mesharshiyya's answer: 'this is written' - the verse says 'as the Lord commanded to do on this day,' referring specifically to the inauguration day.
Rashi glosses 'like this': the Torah required separation only for something structurally identical to the inauguration - not for any case that is merely more sanctified.
Rashi's commentary on 2b ends here mid-word, continuing on 3a with the phrase 'le-inyan' (regarding) - this fragment belongs to the discussion of Rav Ashi's argument.
